serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of curry chicken baked rice is approximately 350 g, comprising a generous portion of buttered baked rice topped with spiced chicken pieces, potato cubes, and curry sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Curry Chicken Baked Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Curry Chicken Baked Rice (cr_id F05100025). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for curry chicken baked rice in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 72 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white rice (jasmine/long-grain, boiled), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The rice component dominates the carbohydrate fraction (20.29 g/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 71.0) / 100 ≈ 51.2, where carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 20.29 × 3.5 = 71.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 256 mg/100g; approximately 896 mg per standard 350 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200 g or fewer, request reduced-salt preparation, and avoid adding additional sauces, as a full serving delivers close to 40% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ≈ 72) with 20.29 g carbohydrate/100g and low dietary fibre (1.79 g/100g) producing a glycemic load of approximately 51 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high glycemic load; substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, increasing non-starchy vegetable content, and limiting the portion to 150 g can substantially reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 3.5 g/100g (predominantly from coconut milk and butter) and sodium 256 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a reduced-portion serving (≤200 g), request light coconut milk preparation to lower saturated fat intake, and pair with a fibre-rich side dish to partially offset the atherogenic lipid and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory curry spices (turmeric, cumin, coriander) with curcumin content; saturated fat below 5 g/100g threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Curry spices provide meaningful anti-inflammatory polyphenols including curcumin; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish in moderate portions while monitoring personal tolerance to nightshade ingredients such as chilli.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Curry Chicken Baked Rice is a Singaporean mamak-style baked rice dish consisting of buttered white rice baked in an oven until lightly golden, topped with tender spiced chicken pieces, soft potato cubes, and a rich curry sauce made from coconut milk and a blend of curry spices including turmeric, cumin, coriander, and garam masala. The dish delivers a fragrant, mildly spicy flavour profile with a creamy, slightly oily texture from the coconut milk-based sauce and butter-enriched rice. It is commonly served as a main meal at mamak stalls and casual dining establishments across Singapore. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 166 kcal, 6.0 g protein, 6.79 g fat (approximately 3.5 g saturated), and 20.29 g carbohydrate with 1.79 g dietary fibre and 256 mg sodium. The high-GI white rice base (GI ≈ 72) produces a high glycemic load per serving, making this dish a concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The sodium content is borderline elevated, requiring portion control for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The anti-inflammatory curry spice blend offers some benefit for inflammatory conditions. The dish is halal-certified and widely available in Singapore's multicultural food landscape.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100025) at confidence 0.95. The dish is a canonical Singapore mamak dish with an exact HPB record. GI is estimated from the closest approved analogous food (white rice, GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database) and is clearly documented as an analogous estimate. Saturated fat (3.5 g/100g) and potassium (180 mg/100g) are carried forward from the reviewed input record. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and micronutrient fields are set to -1 where the HPB source does not report detailed fatty acid or micronutrient breakdowns for this composite dish.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for curry chicken baked rice are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this composite dish as of 2026-04-16. 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 (confirmed negligible/absent per HPB source and consistent with the dish's preparation method). 3. calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this composite dish entry. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (jasmine/long-grain, boiled) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for curry chicken baked rice exists in any approved GI source. 5. Saturated fat value of 3.5 g/100g is carried forward from the reviewed input record (sourced from HPB); this is consistent with the coconut milk and butter content of the dish. 6. Potassium value of 180 mg/100g is carried forward from the reviewed input record. 7. The dish is classified as mamak cuisine, a Singaporean-Indian Muslim culinary tradition; all mandatory multilingual names (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) have been provided.
